title: Asking and Answering Questions
introBody: Asking questions while you read helps you understand a story better. Today we will learn how to ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, and how questions!
introImage: A cartoon child reading a book at a cozy window seat, with colorful question words floating in thought bubbles above.
teachHeading: How Do Questions Help Us Read?
teachBody: Good readers ask questions as they read. Questions like who, what, where, when, why, and how help us find important details in the story. The answers are always right there in the text!
teachImage: A friendly cartoon owl teacher pointing at a chalkboard showing the words Who, What, Where, When, Why, How in colorful chalk.
exampleBody: Read this: Jake's family went camping by the lake on Saturday. They set up a big blue tent and roasted marshmallows. Question: When did Jake's family go camping? Answer: On Saturday! The text told us right there.
exampleImage: A happy cartoon family roasting marshmallows by a campfire next to a blue tent beside a sparkling lake at sunset.
tipBody: When you see a question, look for clue words in the text. If the question asks when, look for time words like today, yesterday, or Saturday. Match the question word to the clue!
tipImage: A cartoon child dressed as a detective with a magnifying glass, circling clue words in a book with a red pen.
practiceBody: You are ready to be a question-answering superstar! Let's practice with some stories. Remember to look for clue words in the text!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a gold star badge that says Reading Star, flexing their arms with confidence, surrounded by sparkles.

title: Finding the Message of a Story
introBody: Stories teach us important lessons about life. Today we will learn how to retell a story and figure out the big message or moral the author wants us to learn!
introImage: A cartoon child sitting cross-legged on a colorful rug, holding an open storybook with a glowing light bulb floating above their head.
teachBody: The central message is the big lesson a story teaches. To find it, think about what the main character learns. Ask yourself: what does this story want me to remember?
teachImage: A cartoon treasure chest opening to reveal a golden scroll that reads Lesson Learned, with sparkles coming out of the chest.
exampleBody: Read this: Bella wanted to win the race all by herself. She refused help from her friends. She tripped and fell. Her friends helped her up and they finished together. Central message: Working together is better than doing everything alone.
exampleImage: Cartoon children helping a girl up after she fell during a race on a sunny school track, all smiling and holding hands.
tipBody: To find the message, look at what happens at the end of the story. Think about how the character changed. The lesson is often about what the character finally understands!
tipImage: A cartoon child thinking with two thought bubbles: one showing a character at the beginning of a story looking grumpy, and one at the end looking happy.
practiceBody: Now it is your turn to find the lesson in each story! Think about what the characters learn and you will find the message every time.
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding up a sign that says I Found the Message with a big proud smile, confetti falling around them.

title: How Characters Handle Challenges
introBody: Characters in stories face problems and exciting events. Today we will learn how to describe what characters do when something big happens in the story!
introImage: A cartoon child standing at a fork in a colorful forest path, looking thoughtful, with one path going over a bridge and the other through a tunnel.
teachHeading: What Do Characters Do When Things Happen?
teachBody: When something important happens in a story, characters react. They might feel scared, brave, or surprised. Pay attention to what they say, do, and feel when big events happen!
teachImage: Three cartoon faces showing different reactions: one surprised with wide eyes, one determined with clenched fists, and one worried biting their lip.
exampleBody: Read this: A big storm knocked down the tree fort. Sam felt sad at first. Then he got his tools and said, "I will build an even better one!" How did Sam respond to the challenge? He decided to rebuild instead of giving up!
exampleImage: A cartoon boy holding a hammer and nails, smiling bravely next to a fallen treehouse, with a new blueprint spread out on the ground.
tipBody: Look for action words and feeling words after a big event. Words like cried, laughed, ran, or decided tell you exactly how the character responded to what happened!
tipImage: A cartoon detective dog sniffing a trail of colorful word footprints that say felt, said, decided, and ran.
practiceBody: Time to watch how characters handle tough moments! Pay attention to what they do and say after something big happens in the story.
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a cape that says Story Hero, standing on top of a book with arms raised in victory.

title: Words That Dance and Sing
introBody: Some words in stories and poems are chosen because they sound fun or make you feel something special. Today we will explore how words give rhythm and meaning to what we read!
introImage: Cartoon musical notes and colorful words like splash, whoosh, and sparkle bouncing around a smiling child who is reading a poem.
teachHeading: How Do Words Create Rhythm and Meaning?
teachBody: Authors pick words carefully. Some words rhyme, some repeat, and some sound like what they mean, like buzz or pop. These choices make stories and poems fun to read out loud!
teachImage: A cartoon author sitting at a desk surrounded by floating word bubbles that rhyme: cat, hat, bat, with musical notes between them.
exampleBody: Read this poem: "The rain came down, drip, drop, drip. It splashed and danced on every tip." The words drip and drop sound like rain! And danced makes the rain feel alive and playful.
exampleImage: Cartoon raindrops with happy faces dancing and splashing on colorful flower petals, with the words drip and drop floating nearby.
tipBody: Read poems and stories out loud! When you hear words that rhyme, repeat, or sound fun, those are the special words the author chose to create rhythm and meaning.
tipImage: A cartoon child reading a book out loud with colorful sound waves coming from their mouth, swirling around the room.
practiceBody: Let's listen for special words! Read each passage out loud and notice which words sound musical or make you feel something. You have a great ear for this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing headphones shaped like books, bobbing their head to the rhythm, with musical notes floating around.

title: Beginning, Middle, and End
introBody: Every story has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Today we will learn how to spot these three parts and understand how they fit together to make a great story!
introImage: A cartoon road split into three colorful sections labeled Beginning, Middle, and End, with a child walking along it holding a book.
teachBody: The beginning introduces the characters and setting. The middle is where the problem or adventure happens. The end is where the problem gets solved. Every story follows this pattern!
teachImage: Three cartoon panels side by side: first showing a girl meeting a puppy, second showing the puppy getting lost, third showing them reunited with a hug.
exampleBody: Beginning: Mia found a magic seed. Middle: She planted it and a giant beanstalk grew overnight. She climbed it and met a friendly giant. End: The giant gave her a golden egg and she climbed home safely.
exampleImage: A cartoon girl climbing a giant green beanstalk that stretches into fluffy clouds, with a friendly giant waving from above.
tipBody: To find the three parts, ask: Who do I meet first? That is the beginning. What is the big problem? That is the middle. How does it all work out? That is the end!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding three puzzle pieces labeled B, M, and E, fitting them together to form a complete picture of a story scene.
practiceBody: You are ready to find the beginning, middle, and end of every story! Think about the three parts as you read and everything will make sense.
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing proudly next to a completed three-part story map on a colorful poster board with gold star stickers.

title: Different Characters, Different Views
introBody: In a story, different characters can think and feel differently about the same thing. Today we will learn to notice how each character has their own special point of view!
introImage: Two cartoon children looking at the same cloud: one sees a dragon shape and the other sees a bunny, with thought bubbles showing their views.
teachBody: Point of view means how a character thinks, feels, and sees things. In a story, one character might be excited about rain while another is upset. Each character speaks in their own voice!
teachImage: A cartoon scene of rain: one child jumping happily in puddles while another child frowns under an umbrella, both watching the same rain.
exampleBody: Read this: Mom said, "What a beautiful garden!" But little brother said, "Yuck, there are bugs everywhere!" Mom and brother see the garden differently. Mom loves it. Brother does not like the bugs!
exampleImage: A cartoon mom smelling flowers happily in a garden while a little boy next to her makes a grossed-out face at a ladybug on a leaf.
tipBody: Pay attention to what each character says and does. Their words and actions show you their point of view. If two characters feel differently, that is a point of view difference!
tipImage: A cartoon split screen showing the same birthday party: one side shows a shy child hiding, the other shows an excited child dancing.
practiceBody: Now let's listen to different characters and figure out how each one feels! Remember, every character has their own way of seeing things.
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding two different masks, one happy and one surprised, pretending to be different characters on a small stage.

title: Pictures and Words Work Together
introHeading: Pictures Tell Part of the Story!
introBody: When you read, the pictures and the words work as a team. Today we will learn how illustrations help us understand characters, settings, and what is happening in a story!
introImage: A cartoon open book lying flat with a colorful jungle scene popping up in 3D from the pages, with a monkey swinging from a vine.
teachHeading: How Do Pictures Help Us Understand?
teachBody: Illustrations show us details the words might not say. A picture can show you how a character feels, what a place looks like, or what is happening in the scene. Always look at both!
teachImage: A cartoon child looking at a book illustration of a snowy cabin, with arrows pointing to details: smoke from chimney, footprints in snow, warm light in window.
exampleBody: The text says: "Lily walked into the room." But the picture shows Lily with wide eyes and her mouth open. Now we know Lily was surprised! The picture gave us extra information the words did not.
exampleImage: A cartoon girl stepping through a doorway into a room full of birthday decorations and presents, her face showing total surprise.
tipBody: After you read the words, always look at the picture. Ask yourself: what does the picture show me that the words did not say? You will discover hidden details every time!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a magnifying glass examining a book illustration closely, with small detail bubbles zooming in on parts of the picture.
practiceBody: Let's use pictures and words together to understand stories! Look carefully at every illustration and you will find extra clues the author left for you.
practiceImage: A cartoon child giving a thumbs up while holding a book open, with the illustration on the page glowing and sparkling.

title: Same Story, Different Versions
introHeading: One Story, Many Ways to Tell It!
introBody: Did you know the same story can be told in different ways? Today we will learn how to compare two versions of a story and find what is the same and what is different!
introImage: Two cartoon books side by side, both showing Cinderella on the cover but in different art styles: one with a glass slipper and one with a golden sandal.
teachHeading: How Can the Same Story Be Different?
teachBody: Different authors or cultures may tell the same story with different characters, settings, or endings. The main idea stays similar, but the details change. Comparing them is like a fun puzzle!
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram on a whiteboard with two overlapping circles labeled Version 1 and Version 2, with a smiling child writing in them.
exampleBody: In one version of The Three Little Pigs, the wolf blows down two houses. In another version from a different country, it is a tiger instead of a wolf! Both stories teach the same lesson about building strong things.
exampleImage: A split scene showing a cartoon wolf on the left blowing at a straw house and a cartoon tiger on the right pushing against a straw hut.
tipBody: When you compare two versions, ask: What is the same? What is different? Look at the characters, the setting, and the ending. Make a list to help you keep track!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding a clipboard with two columns labeled Same and Different, checking items off with a pencil.
practiceBody: Time to be a story detective! Read two versions of a tale and spot the differences and similarities. You are going to be great at this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child sitting between two open books, pointing at both and smiling, with colorful comparison arrows connecting similar parts.

title: Reading All Kinds of Stories
introBody: The more you read, the better you get! Today we will practice reading different kinds of stories, like fairy tales, fables, and adventures. Each one makes you a stronger reader!
introImage: A cartoon child flying on a magic carpet made of book pages over a landscape of different story worlds: a castle, a jungle, and an ocean.
teachHeading: How Does Reading Make You Stronger?
teachBody: Every story you read teaches your brain something new. Some stories are easy and some are tricky, but every one helps you grow. The more you practice, the more you understand!
teachImage: A cartoon brain character lifting a barbell made of books, getting stronger with each rep, with a progress bar above going from beginner to super reader.
exampleBody: Here is a short fable: A crow was thirsty. He found a jar with a little water at the bottom. He dropped pebbles in until the water rose high enough to drink. Smart thinking, crow! See, you just read and understood a whole fable!
exampleImage: A clever cartoon crow dropping small pebbles into a tall glass jar, with the water level slowly rising, in a sunny garden setting.
tipBody: If a story feels hard, do not give up! Read it slowly, look at the pictures, and reread parts that are tricky. Every time you push through, you become a better reader!
tipImage: A cartoon child climbing a mountain made of stacked books, almost at the top, with a flag that says I Can Do It waiting at the peak.
practiceBody: Let's read some fun stories together! Take your time, enjoy every word, and show how much you have grown as a reader. You've got this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child sitting in a cozy reading nook surrounded by a tall stack of finished books, holding the newest one with a proud grin.

title: Questions About Real Things
introBody: When you read about real things like animals, places, or science, asking questions helps you learn more. Today we will practice asking and answering questions about informational text!
introImage: A cartoon child at a library table surrounded by nonfiction books about space, animals, and weather, with question marks floating above.
teachHeading: How Do We Ask Questions About Facts?
teachBody: Just like with stories, we use who, what, where, when, why, and how. But now we are reading about real facts! The answers are right there in the text waiting for you to find them.
teachImage: A friendly cartoon robot teacher holding up flashcards with the question words Who, What, Where, When, Why, and How in different colors.
exampleBody: Read this: Penguins live in cold places like Antarctica. They cannot fly, but they are great swimmers. Question: Where do penguins live? Answer: In cold places like Antarctica! The text told us exactly.
exampleImage: A group of cartoon penguins swimming and sliding on ice in Antarctica, with a snowy mountain and blue ocean in the background.
tipBody: When you read a question about a fact, go back to the text and look for the exact sentence that answers it. The answer is hiding in the words. You just have to find it!
tipImage: A cartoon child using a flashlight to search through lines of text on a page, with one sentence glowing yellow as the answer.
practiceBody: Time to find facts! Read each passage carefully and answer the questions. Remember, the text always has the answers if you look closely.
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a safari hat and holding binoculars, looking at an open nonfiction book as if exploring for facts.

title: Finding the Main Topic
introHeading: What Is This Text All About?
introBody: Every informational text has a main topic, which is the big idea it is mostly about. Today we will learn to find the main topic and see what each paragraph focuses on!
introImage: A cartoon child standing in front of a giant jigsaw puzzle with a big piece in the center labeled Main Topic and smaller pieces around it.
teachHeading: How Do We Find the Main Topic?
teachBody: The main topic is what the whole text is about. Each paragraph adds a piece of information. Ask yourself: if I had to describe this text in one or two words, what would I say?
teachImage: A cartoon umbrella labeled Main Topic with rain drops falling under it, each drop containing a detail word like habitat, food, and size.
exampleBody: Paragraph 1 talks about what dolphins eat. Paragraph 2 talks about where dolphins live. Paragraph 3 talks about how dolphins communicate. The main topic is dolphins! Each paragraph tells us something different about them.
exampleImage: A cartoon dolphin jumping out of the ocean with three small signs in the water labeled Food, Home, and Sounds.
tipBody: Look at what gets mentioned in every paragraph. If the same subject keeps coming up, that is your main topic! Each paragraph zooms in on one part of that topic.
tipImage: A cartoon child looking through a telescope at a page of text, with the main topic word glowing bigger than the rest.
practiceBody: Let's find the big idea! Read each text and ask: what is this mostly about? You are a main topic master now!
practiceImage: A cartoon child planting a flag on top of a mountain of books, with the flag reading Main Topic Found.

title: How Things Are Connected
introBody: In informational texts, events, ideas, and steps are connected to each other. Today we will learn how to see the links between things like history events, science ideas, and how-to steps!
introImage: A cartoon chain made of colorful links, each link containing a small picture: a seed, rain, a sprout, and a flower, showing a connected sequence.
teachHeading: What Are Connections in a Text?
teachBody: Connections show how one thing leads to another. A history text might show cause and effect. A science text might show steps in order. Look for words like first, next, then, and because!
teachImage: A cartoon flowchart on a poster with arrows connecting boxes: Caterpillar, then arrow to Cocoon, then arrow to Butterfly, with a child pointing at it.
exampleBody: Read this: First, you plant the seed. Then, you water it every day. Next, a sprout pops up. Finally, a flower blooms! Each step connects to the next. Without water, the sprout would never grow!
exampleImage: Four cartoon panels showing a child planting a seed, watering it, watching a green sprout, and celebrating a blooming sunflower.
tipBody: Look for signal words like because, so, first, next, and finally. These words are like road signs that show you how the ideas connect to each other!
tipImage: A cartoon road with signposts along it reading First, Next, Then, and Finally, with a child driving a tiny car past them.
practiceBody: Let's connect the dots! Read each passage and look for how the ideas or events link together. You will be a connection expert in no time!
practiceImage: A cartoon child drawing lines between dots on a connect-the-dots page, revealing a picture of a light bulb as the connections form.

title: Figuring Out New Words
introBody: When you read about real topics, you will find new and interesting words. Today we will learn how to figure out what those words mean by looking at the text around them!
introImage: A cartoon child looking at a glowing new word in a book, with a thought bubble showing gears turning as they figure out the meaning.
teachHeading: How Do We Figure Out Word Meanings?
teachBody: The words around a new word give you clues about what it means. This is called context. If a text says animals that hibernate sleep all winter, you can figure out that hibernate means to sleep for a long time!
teachImage: A cartoon magnifying glass hovering over the word hibernate in a sentence, with arrows pointing to the context clue words sleep and winter.
exampleBody: Read this: "The ocean is vast. It stretches as far as you can see in every direction." What does vast mean? The clue is "stretches as far as you can see." Vast means very, very big!
exampleImage: A cartoon child standing on a beach looking out at a huge, endless blue ocean stretching to the horizon in every direction.
tipBody: When you find a word you do not know, read the sentence again slowly. Look for clue words nearby that explain or describe it. The meaning is usually hiding close by!
tipImage: A cartoon child circling an unknown word in a sentence with a crayon, then drawing arrows to helpful clue words nearby.
practiceBody: Time to be a word detective! Find the new words in each passage and use the clues around them to figure out their meanings. You can do it!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a word detective badge, holding a net and catching colorful floating vocabulary words.

title: Cool Text Features
introBody: Informational books have special features like bold words, captions, headings, and glossaries. Today we will learn how these features help you find information faster!
introImage: A cartoon open textbook with colorful arrows pointing to a bold word, a caption under a picture, a subheading, and a glossary at the back.
teachBody: Text features are like signs in a book. Bold print shows important words. Captions explain pictures. Subheadings tell you what a section is about. A glossary is a mini dictionary at the back!
teachImage: A cartoon child in a library holding a book with pop-up labels pointing to features: Bold Word here, Caption here, Glossary here.
exampleBody: Look at this page about frogs. The subheading says "What Frogs Eat." The word insects is in bold. Under the picture it says, "A frog catches a fly with its tongue." Each feature helps you understand the topic!
exampleImage: A cartoon textbook page about frogs with a subheading, a bold word, and a photo of a frog with a caption underneath it.
tipBody: Before you read a whole page, look at the text features first! Read the headings and bold words. This gives your brain a sneak peek at what the text is about.
tipImage: A cartoon child peeking through binoculars at a page, with the bold words and headings glowing brightly while the rest is dimmed.
practiceBody: Let's explore text features! Look for bold words, captions, headings, and glossaries. They are your secret shortcuts to understanding any informational text!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with a treasure map made to look like a book page, with X marks on each text feature they need to find.

title: What the Author Wants to Tell You
introHeading: Why Did the Author Write This?
introBody: Every author has a reason for writing. Some want to explain something, some want to describe, and some want to answer a question. Today we will learn to figure out the author's purpose!
introImage: A cartoon author sitting at a desk with three thought bubbles above: one says Explain, one says Describe, and one says Answer.
teachBody: The author's purpose is why they wrote the text. If it teaches you how something works, the purpose is to explain. If it paints a picture with words, the purpose is to describe. Think about what the author wants you to get!
teachImage: Three cartoon books on a shelf with labels: one says How Volcanoes Work (Explain), one says The Beautiful Rainforest (Describe), one says Why Dogs Bark (Answer).
exampleBody: Read this: "Have you ever wondered why the sky is blue? Sunlight is made of many colors. When it hits the air, blue light scatters the most." The author's purpose is to answer a question: why the sky is blue!
exampleImage: A cartoon child lying on grass looking up at a bright blue sky, with beams of sunlight splitting into rainbow colors overhead.
tipBody: Ask yourself: did this text teach me how to do something, tell me what something looks like, or answer a question I might have? That will reveal the author's purpose!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding three signs shaped like arrows: one pointing to Explain, one to Describe, and one to Answer, choosing the right one.
practiceBody: Let's figure out why each author wrote their text! Think about what the author wants you to learn or understand. You are great at this!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a magnifying glass necklace and pointing at a book, with a light bulb glowing above their head.

title: How Pictures Help Explain
introBody: In informational books, pictures, diagrams, and charts help explain what the words are saying. Today we will learn how images give us extra information and make things easier to understand!
introImage: A cartoon child looking at a colorful diagram of the water cycle in a science book, with arrows pointing from clouds to rain to rivers.
teachHeading: How Do Images Help Us Learn?
teachBody: A picture can show you something that is hard to explain with just words. A photo of a volcano shows you what it really looks like. A diagram shows you how something works. Images and words are a great team!
teachImage: A cartoon split scene: on the left, a paragraph about butterfly wings; on the right, a labeled close-up diagram of a butterfly wing showing the patterns.
exampleBody: The text says: "A caterpillar changes into a butterfly." The diagram next to it shows four stages: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly. The image helps you see all the steps the words did not fully explain!
exampleImage: A colorful cartoon diagram showing the four stages of a butterfly life cycle arranged in a circle with arrows connecting each stage.
tipBody: When you see a picture, chart, or diagram, stop and study it. Ask: what does this image show me that the words did not? Images often give you bonus information!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a checklist comparing Words told me and Picture showed me, checking off items in both columns.
practiceBody: Let's see how images make informational texts better! Look at the pictures carefully and think about what extra information they give you.
practiceImage: A cartoon child high-fiving a book that has a colorful diagram popping out of its pages, both smiling.

title: Reasons That Support a Point
introBody: When an author makes a point, they give reasons to support it. Today we will learn how to find the author's main point and the reasons they use to back it up!
introImage: A cartoon child building a tower of blocks, with the top block labeled Point and the supporting blocks labeled Reason 1, Reason 2, Reason 3.
teachBody: A point is what the author believes or wants to say. Reasons are the facts and examples that explain why the point is true. Without reasons, a point is just a guess!
teachImage: A cartoon table with a sign on top reading Author's Point and three sturdy legs labeled Reason 1, Reason 2, and Reason 3 holding it up.
exampleBody: The author says: "Dogs make great pets." Reason 1: They are loyal and loving. Reason 2: They can learn tricks. Reason 3: They keep you active by going on walks. Each reason supports the point!
exampleImage: A cartoon dog doing tricks, being loyal next to its owner, and going on a walk, shown in three small panels side by side.
tipBody: First find the author's main point. Then look for the word because or phrases like for example and one reason is. Those signal words lead you straight to the supporting reasons!
tipImage: A cartoon child following a trail of breadcrumbs labeled because, for example, and also, leading to a treasure chest of reasons.
practiceBody: Time to find the author's point and reasons! Look for the big idea and the facts that hold it up. You are a reasons-finding champion!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing next to a completed evidence board with a point at the top and reasons connected by string below it, looking proud.

title: Comparing Two Texts on One Topic
introBody: Sometimes you read two different texts about the same topic. Today we will learn how to compare them and find the most important points that are the same or different!
introImage: A cartoon child holding two books, one in each hand, both about planets, comparing them with a thoughtful expression.
teachHeading: How Do We Compare Two Texts?
teachBody: When two texts are about the same topic, they might share some facts but also have different ones. Read both carefully and ask: what do they both say? What does only one of them say?
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram on a big poster board with two overlapping circles, one blue labeled Text 1 and one green labeled Text 2, with shared facts in the middle.
exampleBody: Text 1 says bees make honey and live in hives. Text 2 says bees pollinate flowers and live in hives. Both texts agree that bees live in hives! But one focuses on honey and the other on pollination.
exampleImage: A cartoon split scene: on the left a bee making honey in a hive, on the right a bee pollinating colorful flowers, with a hive in the middle shared by both.
tipBody: Make a simple list or Venn diagram. Write the important points from each text. Circle the ones that match. The matching points are the most important shared ideas!
tipImage: A cartoon child at a desk writing two columns labeled Text 1 and Text 2, circling matching facts with a green crayon.
practiceBody: Let's compare two texts and see what they have in common! This is like being a fact detective. Find the most important points and you will ace it!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a gold medal that says Compare Champ, standing between two open books and pointing at both.

title: Reading to Learn New Things
introBody: Every informational text you read teaches you something new about the world. Today we will practice reading all kinds of nonfiction texts to become stronger, smarter readers!
introImage: A cartoon child sitting on a globe, reading a book, with small images of animals, rockets, and weather swirling around them.
teachBody: Nonfiction texts teach you real facts about the world. The more you read, the more you know! Some texts are easy and some are a little harder, but each one makes your brain grow.
teachImage: A cartoon garden where books are planted like flowers, each bloom showing a topic: animals, space, weather, and history, with a child watering them.
exampleBody: Read this: "Sea turtles can live for more than 80 years. They travel thousands of miles through the ocean." You just learned two cool facts about sea turtles! See how reading teaches you new things?
exampleImage: A cartoon sea turtle swimming through a beautiful blue ocean, with a dotted line showing its long journey path across the water.
tipBody: If a text feels hard, slow down and reread it. Try to say the main idea in your own words. If you can explain it, you understand it! Practice makes you a reading champion.
tipImage: A cartoon child rereading a paragraph with a calm smile, with a slow-down sign next to them and a thought bubble showing understanding.
practiceBody: Let's read and learn! Take your time with each text and soak up all the interesting facts. You are becoming a nonfiction reading pro!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing on a podium with a trophy that says Reading Pro, surrounded by nonfiction books and cheering classmates.

title: Cracking the Code of Words
introBody: Decoding means using what you know about letters and sounds to read words. Today we will practice our phonics skills to crack the code of new words!
introImage: A cartoon child wearing a code-breaker helmet, looking at a word through a special decoder device that lights up each letter.
teachBody: When you see a new word, break it into parts. Look at the letters and say their sounds. Then blend the sounds together to read the whole word. You are a word decoder!
teachImage: A cartoon word being broken apart into colored letter blocks: the word sunshine split into sun and shine, with arrows showing them coming together.
exampleBody: Try this word: jumping. First, find the parts: jump and ing. Say each part: jump, ing. Now put them together: jumping! You decoded it! Breaking words into parts makes them easy to read.
exampleImage: A cartoon child holding two puzzle pieces that say jump and ing, clicking them together with a satisfying snap to form jumping.
tipBody: If a word looks long and tricky, do not panic! Cover part of it with your finger and read one chunk at a time. Then put the chunks together. You have got this!
tipImage: A cartoon hand using a finger to cover part of a long word, revealing one chunk at a time, with a smiling child peeking over.
practiceBody: Time to decode some words! Break them into parts, sound them out, and blend them together. You are a phonics superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon child flexing their muscles next to a cracked-open word safe, with decoded words spilling out like treasures.

title: Long and Short Vowels
introBody: Did you know that vowels can say their short sound or their long sound? Today we will learn how to tell the difference when we read one-syllable words!
introImage: A cartoon set of five vowel letters A, E, I, O, U standing on a stage, each with two faces: one short (quick) and one long (saying their name).
teachBody: A short vowel makes a quick sound, like the a in cat. A long vowel says its name, like the a in cake. The silent e at the end often makes the vowel say its name!
teachImage: A cartoon split chalkboard: left side shows cat with a short a sound wave, right side shows cake with a long a sound wave and a silent e highlighted.
exampleBody: Look at these two words: hop and hope. In hop, the o makes a short sound. In hope, the silent e makes the o say its name. Short o: hop. Long o: hope. One little e changes everything!
exampleImage: A cartoon bunny hopping (labeled hop) next to a cartoon child making a wish on a star (labeled hope), showing the two different meanings.
tipBody: Check the end of the word! If it ends with a consonant, the vowel is usually short. If it ends with a silent e, the vowel is usually long. This trick works most of the time!
tipImage: A cartoon child looking at the end of a word with a magnifying glass, with a speech bubble saying Silent e? Long vowel!
practiceBody: Let's sort some words into short and long vowel groups! Listen to the vowel sound carefully and you will know right away which group it belongs in.
practiceImage: A cartoon child sorting word cards into two colorful bins labeled Short Vowel and Long Vowel, tossing a card into the right bin.

title: Vowel Teams Stick Together
introBody: Sometimes two vowels stand next to each other and make one sound together. These are called vowel teams! Today we will learn common vowel teams like ai, ea, oa, and more!
introImage: Cartoon letter pairs ai, ea, oa, and oo holding hands like best friends, standing on a stage under a banner that says Vowel Teams.
teachBody: When two vowels team up, usually the first one says its long name and the second one stays quiet. For example, in the word rain, the a says its name and the i is silent. Two vowels go walking, the first one does the talking!
teachImage: A cartoon pair of vowels a and i walking together on a path, with the a talking in a speech bubble and the i holding a quiet sign.
exampleBody: Read these words: boat, team, and mail. In boat, oa says long o. In team, ea says long e. In mail, ai says long a. The first vowel talks and the second one stays quiet!
exampleImage: Three cartoon scenes: a boat on water, a sports team cheering, and a mailbox with a letter, each with the vowel team highlighted and glowing.
tipBody: When you see two vowels next to each other, try saying the first vowel's long sound. If the word sounds right, you found the vowel team! This rule works for most common pairs.
tipImage: A cartoon child pointing at two vowels in a word and saying the first one's name, with a green check mark appearing above.
practiceBody: Let's practice reading vowel team words! Remember, the first vowel does the talking. You will be a vowel team expert in no time!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a coach whistle and jersey, leading a team of vowel letter pairs through a reading obstacle course.

title: Two-Syllable Words with Long Vowels
introBody: Some words have two syllables and long vowel sounds. Today we will learn how to break these bigger words into smaller parts and read them with confidence!
introImage: A cartoon child karate-chopping a long word into two syllable pieces, with each piece glowing in a different color.
teachHeading: How Do We Read Two-Syllable Words?
teachBody: First, split the word into two syllables. Then look for long vowel patterns like silent e or vowel teams in each part. Read each syllable, then blend them together to say the whole word!
teachImage: A cartoon word cupcake being sliced in half by a friendly knife, showing cup and cake as two separate syllables with the silent e highlighted.
exampleBody: Try the word pancake. Split it: pan-cake. The first part is pan. The second part is cake, with a long a because of the silent e. Put them together: pancake! You just decoded a two-syllable word!
exampleImage: A cartoon stack of golden pancakes on a plate being split into two word halves: pan on the left stack and cake on the right stack.
tipBody: Clap the word to find the syllables! Each clap is one syllable. Then read each syllable by itself. Finally, say them together fast. Clap, read, blend!
tipImage: A cartoon child clapping along while reading the word sunshine, with two clap marks shown: sun (clap) and shine (clap).
practiceBody: Let's tackle some two-syllable words! Break them apart, find the long vowel sounds, and put them back together. You are a syllable superstar!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing on a winner's podium holding a trophy shaped like the word cupcake, celebrating with confetti.

title: Prefixes and Suffixes
introBody: Prefixes and suffixes are small word parts that get added to words. They change how a word sounds and what it means! Today we will learn to spot them and decode words that have them.
introImage: A cartoon base word happy with the prefix un- snapping on like a puzzle piece to the front, turning it into unhappy.
teachBody: A prefix goes at the beginning of a word, like un- or re-. A suffix goes at the end, like -ful or -less. When you spot these parts, take them off to find the base word, then add the meaning back!
teachImage: A cartoon word factory with a conveyor belt: the base word play enters, then a suffix -ful is attached, and playful comes out the other side.
exampleBody: Look at the word unkind. Take off the prefix un-. The base word is kind. Un- means not. So unkind means not kind! Now try rereading: re- means again, read is the base, so reread means to read again.
exampleImage: A cartoon child peeling off the prefix un- from the word unkind like a sticker, revealing the base word kind underneath.
tipBody: When you see a long word, look for prefixes at the start and suffixes at the end. Peel them away to find the base word. Then put the meaning back together like a puzzle!
tipImage: A cartoon word puzzle with three pieces: re- on the left, play in the middle, and -ing on the right, being assembled by a child.
practiceBody: Let's find prefixes and suffixes in words! Peel them apart, decode the base word, and put the meaning together. You are a word-building wizard!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a wizard hat, waving a wand that adds prefixes and suffixes to floating words, transforming them.

title: Tricky Spelling Patterns
introBody: Some words have letter combinations that do not follow the usual rules. Today we will learn to recognize tricky spelling patterns that can make different sounds in different words!
introImage: A cartoon group of letters wearing disguise masks, like the letters ough shown four times making different sounds in through, though, tough, and cough.
teachBody: English has some letter patterns that can make more than one sound. For example, ea can sound like long e in bead or short e in bread. When you meet these, try both sounds and see which one makes a real word!
teachImage: A cartoon fork in the road with a sign showing the letters ea, and two paths: one leading to long e (bead, team) and one to short e (bread, head).
exampleBody: Look at the word head. If you try long e, it sounds like heed. That does not sound right! Try short e instead: head. That is a real word! When the first sound does not work, try another.
exampleImage: A cartoon child trying on two hats labeled long e and short e, with the short e hat fitting perfectly on their head.
tipBody: If a word does not sound right the first way you try it, do not worry! Flip the vowel sound and try again. Also, the more you read, the more you will recognize these tricky words by sight!
tipImage: A cartoon child flipping a coin labeled Try Again on one side and Got It on the other, standing next to a tricky word.
practiceBody: Let's practice reading words with tricky spellings! Try different sounds and see which one makes a real word. You are getting better at this every day!
practiceImage: A cartoon child cracking a safe with tricky words inside, spinning the dial labeled Sound 1 and Sound 2 to unlock each word.

title: Words That Break the Rules
introBody: Some words are spelled in ways that do not follow the regular phonics rules. These are called irregularly spelled words. Today we will learn to recognize and read these tricky words!
introImage: A cartoon lineup of words like said, would, friend, and does wearing rebel outfits like sunglasses and leather jackets, looking mischievous.
teachBody: Irregularly spelled words do not sound the way they look. The word said looks like it should rhyme with paid, but it sounds like sed! You just have to remember how these special words sound.
teachImage: A cartoon hall of fame wall with framed irregular words: said, could, were, friend, and love, each with a small note showing their pronunciation.
exampleBody: Look at the word friend. If you sound it out, it might seem like free-end. But it actually sounds like frend! The i and e are tricky in this word. Once you learn it, you will always remember!
exampleImage: Two cartoon children who are best friends, holding a banner that spells friend, with the ie letters wearing tiny crowns for being tricky.
tipBody: The best way to learn irregular words is to practice reading them often. Make flashcards or play a matching game. The more you see these words, the faster you will recognize them!
tipImage: A cartoon child playing a memory matching card game with irregular words, flipping over cards and finding matches with a smile.
practiceBody: Let's practice reading these tricky words! The more you see them, the easier they get. Soon you will read them without even thinking!
practiceImage: A cartoon child zooming past a row of irregular words on a skateboard, reading each one effortlessly as they pass by.

title: Reading Smoothly and Clearly
introBody: Good readers read smoothly, clearly, and with the right speed. This is called fluency! Today we will learn how reading fluently helps you understand what you are reading.
introImage: A cartoon child sitting in a cozy chair reading aloud smoothly, with words flowing out of the book in a colorful, connected stream.
teachBody: Fluency means reading accurately, at a good speed, and with expression. When you read fluently, you do not stop and start. The words flow together and the story makes sense in your head!
teachImage: A cartoon speedometer with three sections labeled Accuracy, Speed, and Expression, with the needle pointing to the green Perfect zone.
exampleBody: Listen to two ways to read: "The. Dog. Ran. Fast." That is too choppy! Now try: "The dog ran fast!" That sounds smooth and natural. When you read smoothly, you understand the meaning better.
exampleImage: Two cartoon panels: one showing a child reading choppily with broken word bubbles, the other showing a child reading smoothly with a flowing sentence ribbon.
tipBody: Practice rereading your favorite pages. The second and third time you read something, your brain already knows the words, so you can focus on reading smoothly and with expression!
tipImage: A cartoon child rereading a book page for the third time, with a progress bar above going from choppy to smooth to expressive.
practiceBody: Time to practice reading smoothly! Read each passage at a nice, steady pace. You will sound amazing!
practiceImage: A cartoon child reading into a microphone on a small stage, with an audience of stuffed animals listening and clapping.

title: Reading with Purpose
introHeading: Read with a Goal in Mind!
introBody: When you read with a purpose, you know what you are looking for. It helps you understand the text better! Today we will practice reading to find answers and make meaning.
introImage: A cartoon child holding a flashlight aimed at an open book, with the beam highlighting important words on the page.
teachHeading: What Does It Mean to Read with Purpose?
teachBody: Reading with purpose means having a reason for reading. Maybe you want to find out what happens next, learn a fact, or answer a question. When you know your purpose, your brain pays closer attention!
teachImage: A cartoon brain character wearing running shoes, jogging toward a finish line flag labeled Understanding, with the starting line labeled Purpose.
exampleBody: Your purpose is: find out what the fox eats. Now read: "The red fox hunts at night. It eats mice, berries, and insects." Because you knew what to look for, you found the answer quickly: mice, berries, and insects!
exampleImage: A cartoon fox eating berries in a moonlit forest, with mice and insects nearby, and a thought bubble showing the child's purpose question.
tipBody: Before you start reading, ask yourself: what am I looking for? Keep that question in your mind as you read. It is like having a map that guides you through the text!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding a small map labeled My Purpose, following a trail through a forest of text to find the answer treasure.
practiceBody: Let's read with a purpose! Before each passage, think about what you want to find out. That will make reading easier and more fun!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with a headband labeled Focused Reader, sitting at a desk with determined eyes, ready to read and find answers.

title: Reading Out Loud with Expression
introBody: When you read out loud, your voice can make the story exciting! Today we will practice reading with the right speed, accuracy, and expression to bring stories to life!
introImage: A cartoon child reading dramatically from a book, with colorful sound effects popping out: BOOM, WHOOSH, and whisper shown in different sizes.
teachBody: Expressive reading means using your voice to show feelings. Read faster during exciting parts and slower during serious parts. Change your voice for different characters. Make the story come alive!
teachImage: A cartoon child reading to a group of friends, making a scary face for the villain part and a sweet voice for the princess, with the friends captivated.
exampleBody: Read this with expression: "Help!" cried the little mouse. "Do not worry," said the brave lion gently. "I will save you." Did you read the mouse's words quickly and the lion's words slowly and calmly?
exampleImage: A cartoon tiny mouse calling for help from a ledge while a gentle, brave lion reaches up to help, both showing clear emotions.
tipBody: Look at punctuation marks for clues! An exclamation point means excitement or strong feeling. A question mark means curiosity. A period means a calm stop. Let punctuation guide your voice!
tipImage: A cartoon set of punctuation marks as characters: an excited exclamation point jumping, a curious question mark scratching its chin, and a calm period sitting still.
practiceBody: Time to read out loud with feeling! Use your voice to show the emotions in each passage. You are going to sound like a real storyteller!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a storyteller's hat, reading from a book on a stage with a spotlight, as the audience leans in with wide eyes.

title: Fixing Mistakes as You Read
introBody: Even great readers make mistakes sometimes. The important thing is knowing how to fix them! Today we will learn how to use the words around a tricky word to check if we read it right.
introImage: A cartoon child rereading a sentence, erasing a wrong word with a magic eraser and replacing it with the correct one, smiling.
teachBody: When a word does not sound right or make sense, stop and try again. Look at the letters more carefully. Think about what would make sense in the sentence. Then reread to check!
teachImage: A cartoon traffic light with three steps: Red says Stop (something is wrong), Yellow says Think (check the word), Green says Go (reread with the right word).
exampleBody: You read: "The cat sat on the mat and drank some milk." But wait, you accidentally read mat as map. A cat does not sit on a map! Reread: mat makes sense. You just self-corrected!
exampleImage: A cartoon cat sitting on a cozy mat next to a bowl of milk, with the word mat circled in green and the wrong word map crossed out in red.
tipBody: Ask yourself two questions: Does this word look right? Does this word make sense? If the answer to either is no, go back and try the word again. Trust your brain!
tipImage: A cartoon child with two thought bubbles: one says Looks right? with a magnifying glass icon, the other says Makes sense? with a light bulb icon.
practiceBody: Let's practice catching and fixing mistakes! Remember, self-correcting is a superpower that all great readers use. You can do it!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a superhero cape labeled Self-Corrector, flying through the air and fixing words in sentences below.

title: Unlocking Unknown Words
introBody: Sometimes you meet words you have never seen before. That is okay! Today we will learn different strategies to figure out what unknown words mean, like a real word detective!
introImage: A cartoon child with a detective hat and magnifying glass, investigating a giant glowing word that has a question mark above it.
teachHeading: How Do We Figure Out Unknown Words?
teachBody: There are many ways to figure out a word! You can use context clues from the sentence, look at word parts like prefixes and suffixes, or use a glossary or dictionary. Each strategy is a tool in your toolbox!
teachImage: A cartoon toolbox labeled Word Tools, with tools inside: a magnifying glass labeled Context Clues, a wrench labeled Word Parts, and a book labeled Dictionary.
exampleBody: You read: "The enormous elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo." You do not know enormous, but the sentence says biggest. That is your clue! Enormous means very, very big.
exampleImage: A cartoon enormous elephant standing next to tiny animals at a zoo, with a child looking up in amazement at how big it is.
tipBody: Try all your strategies! First, read the sentence for clues. Then look at the word parts. If you are still unsure, check a glossary or dictionary. One of these tools will always help!
tipImage: A cartoon child trying three keys on a locked word: the first key labeled Context, the second Word Parts, and the third Dictionary, with the lock clicking open.
practiceBody: Time to use your word detective skills! Try different strategies to figure out each unknown word. You have all the tools you need!
practiceImage: A cartoon child opening a treasure chest full of new vocabulary words, each glowing with its meaning written underneath.

title: Context Clues in Sentences
introBody: When you find a word you do not know, the other words in the sentence can help you figure it out. These are called context clues! Today we will practice using them like a pro.
introImage: A cartoon sentence on a chalkboard with one word glowing and mystery clue arrows pointing to it from the other words in the sentence.
teachBody: Context clues are the words and sentences around an unknown word that give you hints about its meaning. The sentence is like a puzzle, and the clues help you fill in the missing piece!
teachImage: A cartoon jigsaw puzzle with most pieces filled in and one missing piece that is an unknown word, with nearby pieces providing hints.
exampleBody: Read this: "The baby was famished, so she ate a huge bowl of oatmeal." What does famished mean? She ate a huge bowl, so she must have been really hungry! Famished means very hungry.
exampleImage: A cartoon baby eagerly eating a big bowl of oatmeal with a hungry expression, an empty bowl waiting for more, sitting in a high chair.
tipBody: When you find a mystery word, read the whole sentence again. Look for clue words that describe, explain, or give an example. Those clues will point you to the meaning!
tipImage: A cartoon child highlighting clue words in a sentence with a yellow marker, with dotted lines connecting them to the unknown word.
practiceBody: Let's use context clues to crack the code of new words! Read the sentences carefully and let the clue words guide you. You are a context clue champion!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding a golden key labeled Context Clues, unlocking a door that reveals a room full of new words and their meanings.

title: Prefixes Change Word Meanings
introHeading: A Little Part Makes a Big Change!
introBody: A prefix is a small word part added to the beginning of a word that changes its meaning. Today we will learn how to figure out what a new word means when a prefix is added!
introImage: A cartoon prefix un- riding a skateboard and crashing into the word happy, transforming it into unhappy with a poof of colorful smoke.
teachBody: If you know what the prefix means and what the base word means, you can figure out the new word! The prefix un- means not. So unhappy means not happy. The prefix re- means again. So replay means play again!
teachImage: A cartoon math-style equation: un- plus kind equals unkind (not kind), and re- plus build equals rebuild (build again), shown on a whiteboard.
exampleBody: What does the word retell mean? The prefix re- means again. The base word is tell. So retell means to tell again! You just used the prefix to figure out a new word. Great job!
exampleImage: A cartoon child telling a story to friends, then rewinding like a video and telling it again, with the prefix re- shown as a rewind button.
tipBody: Learn a few common prefixes and you can unlock many new words! Un- means not, re- means again, pre- means before, and dis- means not or opposite. These four unlock tons of words!
tipImage: A cartoon key ring with four keys labeled un-, re-, pre-, and dis-, unlocking a word vault filled with new vocabulary.
practiceBody: Let's practice figuring out words with prefixes! Find the prefix, find the base word, and put the meaning together. You are a prefix pro!
practiceImage: A cartoon child stacking blocks: the bottom block says happy, and they are placing a block labeled un- on top, creating a new word tower.

title: Root Words Give You Clues
introBody: Many words share the same root word. If you know one word in the family, you can figure out the others! Today we will use root words as clues to understand new words.
introImage: A cartoon tree with the root word play at its trunk and branches showing playful, replay, player, and playground as leaves.
teachBody: A root word is the main part of a word that gives it its basic meaning. The word teach is a root. Teacher, teaching, and reteach all come from the same root! Knowing the root helps you understand the whole family.
teachImage: A cartoon family portrait of words all sharing the root help: helper, helpful, helpless, and unhelpful, each dressed slightly differently.
exampleBody: You know the word paint. Now you see the word painter. The root is paint, and -er means a person who does something. So a painter is a person who paints! The root word gave you the biggest clue.
exampleImage: A cartoon artist (painter) holding a paintbrush and palette next to a colorful canvas, with the root word paint glowing at the center.
tipBody: When you see a long word, look for a smaller word hiding inside it. That smaller word is often the root! Once you find it, the meaning of the bigger word becomes much clearer.
tipImage: A cartoon child using an X-ray machine to look inside a long word, revealing the glowing root word hidden in the middle.
practiceBody: Let's find root words and use them as clues! Look for the familiar word hiding inside each new word. Your root word skills are amazing!
practiceImage: A cartoon child digging in a garden and pulling up root words from the soil, each root growing into a family of related words above ground.

title: Compound Words Are Two in One
introBody: A compound word is made by putting two smaller words together. When you know both small words, you can figure out what the big word means! Today we will become compound word experts!
introImage: Two cartoon puzzle pieces labeled sun and flower clicking together to form the word sunflower, with a sunflower blooming above.
teachBody: Compound words combine two words to make a new one. The meaning of the new word usually comes from combining both meanings. Sunflower is a flower that faces the sun! It is like math for words.
teachImage: A cartoon math equation showing: rain + bow = rainbow, with a rain cloud plus a bow equaling a colorful rainbow arching across the sky.
exampleBody: What does starfish mean? Split it: star + fish. A starfish is a sea creature shaped like a star! What about bedroom? Bed + room. A bedroom is a room with a bed. Easy when you split them up!
exampleImage: A cartoon starfish on the ocean floor next to a star shape, showing how the two words combine, with bubbly underwater scenery.
tipBody: When you see a long word, check if you can split it into two real words. If you can, think about what each word means separately. Then combine those meanings to understand the compound word!
tipImage: A cartoon child using a karate chop to split the word notebook into note and book, with both halves glowing.
practiceBody: Let's split compound words and figure out their meanings! Break each word in two and put the meanings together. You are a compound word champion!
practiceImage: A cartoon child juggling compound word pieces in the air: cup and cake, rain and coat, pop and corn, catching them perfectly.

title: Using Glossaries and Dictionaries
introBody: Glossaries and dictionaries are amazing tools for finding out what words mean. Today we will learn how to use them to look up words and become even better readers!
introImage: A cartoon child opening a giant dictionary like a treasure map, with golden word definitions shining out from the pages.
teachHeading: How Do We Use Glossaries and Dictionaries?
teachBody: A glossary is at the back of a book and defines important words from that book. A dictionary has thousands of words in alphabetical order. Both tell you what words mean and how to use them!
teachImage: A cartoon split image: on the left, a book open to a glossary at the back with highlighted terms; on the right, a thick dictionary with alphabet tabs on the side.
exampleBody: You want to know what habitat means. Open the glossary and look under H. You find: "Habitat: the natural home of an animal or plant." Now you know! A forest is a habitat for deer.
exampleImage: A cartoon child running a finger down a glossary page to the word habitat, with a light bulb turning on above their head.
tipBody: Words in glossaries and dictionaries are in ABC order. Think about the first letter of your word and go to that section. If two words start with the same letter, look at the second letter!
tipImage: A cartoon child sliding down a giant alphabet slide, stopping at the right letter section to find their word.
practiceBody: Let's practice using glossaries and dictionaries! Look up each word and find its meaning. You will be a look-it-up expert in no time!
practiceImage: A cartoon child at a desk with a dictionary and glossary open, giving a confident thumbs up as they find a word's meaning.

title: How Words Are Related
introBody: Words have relationships with each other. Some words are similar, some are opposite, and some have special connections. Today we will explore how words are related and what makes them different!
introImage: A cartoon word family tree with the word happy at the top and related words like glad, joyful, cheerful, and excited branching out below.
teachBody: Some words mean almost the same thing, like big and large. Some words are opposites, like hot and cold. Understanding these relationships helps you pick the perfect word every time!
teachImage: A cartoon seesaw with the word hot on one side and cold on the other, perfectly balanced, with a child in the middle learning about opposites.
exampleBody: Think about these words: tiny, small, little. They are all related because they describe something that is not big. But tiny is the smallest of all! Knowing the differences helps you be a better writer too.
exampleImage: Three cartoon dogs in a row: a very tiny chihuahua, a small terrier, and a little beagle, each labeled with their size word.
tipBody: When you learn a new word, think of words that are similar and words that are opposite. Making these connections helps your brain remember the word and understand it more deeply!
tipImage: A cartoon brain with sticky notes attached, each connecting a new word to similar words on one side and opposite words on the other.
practiceBody: Let's explore word relationships! Think about how words connect, compare, and contrast. Your vocabulary is about to grow so much!
practiceImage: A cartoon child building a web of connected words on a colorful poster, with strings linking related words together.

title: Words in Real Life
introBody: The words we read are connected to real life! Today we will practice connecting words to real-life experiences. When you think about how a word relates to your life, you remember it better!
introImage: A cartoon child pointing at things in their neighborhood: a cozy house, a playful dog, and a tall tree, with word labels floating next to each.
teachHeading: How Do Words Connect to Real Life?
teachBody: Every word connects to something real. When you read the word chilly, think of a cold winter morning. When you read brave, think of a time you were courageous. Real-life connections make words stick!
teachImage: A cartoon child with thought bubbles connecting words to real memories: delicious linked to pizza, exhausted linked to running a race.
exampleBody: The word cozy means warm and comfortable. Can you think of something cozy? Maybe your favorite blanket or a warm cup of cocoa. When you connect cozy to something real, you truly understand it!
exampleImage: A cartoon child wrapped in a fluffy blanket on a sofa, holding a cup of hot cocoa, with a cat curled up next to them by a fireplace.
tipBody: When you learn a new word, ask yourself: when have I seen, felt, or experienced this? Connecting words to your own life is the best way to remember what they mean!
tipImage: A cartoon child drawing a line from a vocabulary word on a flashcard to a drawing of their own real-life experience.
practiceBody: Let's connect words to real life! Think about when and where you have experienced each word. Making connections is your superpower!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing in the middle of a web of words, each connected to a real-life scene like playground, kitchen, and park.

title: Shades of Word Meaning
introBody: Some words mean almost the same thing but have slightly different strengths. Today we will learn how to tell the difference between similar words like walk, jog, and sprint!
introImage: A cartoon paint palette with three shades of blue getting darker, each labeled with a word: chilly, cold, and freezing.
teachBody: Shades of meaning are small differences between similar words. For example, happy, glad, and thrilled all describe good feelings, but thrilled is the strongest! Choosing the right shade makes your language more precise.
teachImage: A cartoon thermometer showing feelings from bottom to top: content, happy, excited, thrilled, with each level getting more intense.
exampleBody: Look at these words: whisper, talk, shout. They all mean to use your voice, but at different volumes. Whisper is the quietest. Talk is normal. Shout is the loudest! Those are shades of meaning.
exampleImage: Three cartoon children side by side: one whispering softly with tiny sound waves, one talking normally, and one shouting with big bold sound waves.
tipBody: When you see two words that seem similar, think about which one is stronger or weaker. Try putting them in order from least to most. That helps you see the shades of meaning clearly!
tipImage: A cartoon child arranging word cards on a number line from weakest to strongest: tap, hit, slam, in order of increasing force.
practiceBody: Let's sort words by their shades of meaning! Put similar words in order from weakest to strongest. You have a great eye for the differences!
practiceImage: A cartoon child painting a gradient of colors on a canvas, with each shade labeled with a word that gets stronger in meaning.

title: Growing Your Word Power
introBody: You learn new words every day from talking, reading, and listening. Today we will practice using all the amazing words you have been collecting to become an even better reader and speaker!
introImage: A cartoon child catching colorful words floating in the air like butterflies with a net, in a garden full of blooming word flowers.
teachHeading: How Do We Grow Our Vocabulary?
teachBody: Every time you read a book, have a conversation, or hear someone read to you, your brain picks up new words. The more words you know, the better you can read, write, and share your ideas!
teachImage: A cartoon brain character watering a garden of word plants, with new vocabulary sprouting up like colorful flowers and growing taller.
exampleBody: In class you learned the word observe, which means to watch carefully. Now when you read a science book that says "observe the butterfly," you know exactly what to do! Your word power just grew!
exampleImage: A cartoon child observing a butterfly through a magnifying glass in a sunny meadow, taking notes in a small notebook.
tipBody: Keep a list of new words you hear or read. Write the word and what it means. Try using it in a sentence. The more you use a new word, the better you remember it!
tipImage: A cartoon child writing in a colorful Word Journal, adding a new word to a growing list, with a pencil and stickers nearby.
practiceBody: Let's use your growing vocabulary! Read each passage and use the words you have been learning. You have more word power than you think!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing tall on a stack of vocabulary books, wearing a crown labeled Word King, with a sunrise behind them.
